![SETAC Logo](/assets/img/logo.svg)

# Beyond Life Cycle Assessment for a More Informed Decision-Making

##### Giuseppe Cecere, Lucia Rigamonti, Politecnico di Milano

In our pursuit of sustainability, we face a mix of environmental, economic and social factors, all shaped by cultural, political and regulatory influences. While we have made progress, finding the best ways to measure sustainability is still a challenge. That is where the session “LCA and Beyond - Integrating Sustainability and/or Other Dimensions for a More Informed Decision-Making“ at the SETAC Europe 34th Annual Meeting comes in. From integrating life cycle assessment (LCA) with traditional risk-based methods to incorporating techno-economic analyses into cradle-to-gate models, various efforts have been made to grasp the multifaceted nature of sustainability. Yet, achieving consensus on the most effective methodologies remains a hurdle, as demonstrated by the ongoing debate surrounding life cycle sustainability assessment (LCSA).

The session, held on Wednesday, 8 May, in Seville, aimed to pave the way for advancements in the evolving landscape of sustainability assessment and provided a platform for researchers to share advancements and experiences with LCA combined with new tools. For example, we discussed the integration of techno-economic analysis with LCA, as a way to enable a deeper understanding of the environmental and economic impacts of production processes. Innovative tools, such as semi-automated platforms, facilitate consistent assessments and reveal crucial trade-offs in sustainable fuel production.

Another noteworthy development highlighted was the European Commission’s Safe and Sustainable by Design (SSbD) framework, which represents a paradigm shift in how we approach the evaluation of chemicals and materials. This framework pushes for the integration of risk assessment (RA) with LCA to create a comprehensive evaluation that considers both safety and sustainability from the outset. This holistic approach is exemplified by BASF’s TripleS methodology, which incorporates human toxicity assessments into LCAs, providing a clearer picture of the direct and indirect impacts of chemicals and paving the way for more informed decision-making in product development and portfolio management.

In the realm of food production, incorporating nutritional aspects and planetary boundaries into LCA is crucial for ensuring sustainable practices. By considering the nutritional value of food products alongside their environmental impacts, we can promote healthier and more sustainable food systems. This holistic approach helps address the complex interplay between food production, health and environmental sustainability, ensuring that our food systems are resilient and capable of meeting the needs of future generations.

The discussion also delved into the potential role of biofuels in improving the sustainability of the transportation sector. This topic has gained attention due to the promising benefits biofuels offer in reducing carbon emissions. Evaluating various dimensions of sustainability, including economic and social factors alongside environmental impacts, has been a key focus. By considering different aspects ranging from cost to global warming, researchers have gained valuable insights into the impacts of biofuel sustainability. This comprehensive analysis could provide stakeholders and policymakers with a clearer understanding of the potential benefits and challenges associated with biofuel integration into the transportation sector, guiding efforts to promote sustainable practices and policies.

The session also highlighted the importance of balancing innovation with long-term environmental and social goals. A study on the fashion industry, where we see how growth strategies can sometimes hinder progress towards sustainability, was presented. Businesses often aim for continuous revenue growth through innovation, but this can inadvertently worsen environmental and social issues. By combining social LCA with LCA, we gain valuable insights into the environmental and social impacts, helping us weigh the trade-offs between profitability and sustainability efforts.

The societal life cycle costing (sLCC), and its application in the context of eco-design policies developed by the European Commission, were also important areas of focus. Specifically, sLCC was used in a broader framework designed to establish and prioritize eco-design requirements for various product groups.

Cutting-edge applications in the field of LCSA methodology couldn't be overlooked. The LCSA framework mirrors LCA in its structure and incorporates stakeholder involvement throughout the process in order to evaluate the impacts on all three dimensions of sustainability. It covers the entire life cycle of the product or the process and employs multi-criteria evaluation. Stakeholders play a crucial role in goal definition, impact category selection and data collection. Additionally, the LCSA integrates social and economic assessments guided by stakeholder engagement, aligning with responsible research and innovation principles. A case study on protein production from microalgae illustrates the practical application of the LCSA framework.

Additionally, the session featured numerous posters that facilitated discussions on integrating various methodologies with life cycle assessment. These posters encouraged critical reflection on a wide range of topics, highlighting the importance of developing life cycle thinking methodologies across different dimensions and possibilities.

In conclusion, the integration of diverse methodologies and approaches in sustainability assessment is essential for developing a comprehensive understanding of the multifaceted nature of sustainability. By embracing holistic and integrated approaches, we can navigate the complexities of sustainability and make informed decisions that promote sustainability across all dimensions, not only the environmental one. The discussion will continue in one of the next LCA discussion forums.

# How to Use LCA for Better Business Decisions?

### Embracing Life Cycle Thinking for Sustainable Decision-Making

In today's rapidly evolving business landscape, where environmental responsibility and sustainability have become a must, organizations are increasingly recognizing the value of Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) as a powerful decision-support tool.

LCA is a comprehensive methodology that examines the environmental impact of a product or service throughout its entire life cycle, from raw material extraction to end-of-life disposal or recycling. By adopting a holistic, cradle-to-grave approach, LCA empowers organizations to make informed, data-driven decisions that minimize their environmental footprint and contribute to a more sustainable future.

Traditionally, LCA has been perceived as a complex and resource-intensive process, reserved for specialized sustainability experts. However, as the availability of user-friendly LCA software and comprehensive databases continues to grow, the barriers to entry have significantly diminished. With competent people and access to the right tools, it is now easier than ever before to leverage LCA for day-to-day decision support, enabling organizations of all sizes to integrate environmental considerations into their everyday operations.

With skilled people and the right LCA software and databases, using LCA for daily decision-making can be easier than you might expect.

### Decisions based on an LCA

Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a powerful methodology that can be applied to a wide range of business decisions such as:

product design and development

supply chain optimization

strategic planning and goal setting

stakeholder engagement and reporting

#### **Product Design and Development**

One of the most impactful applications of LCA is in the product design and development phase. By conducting an LCA early in the product lifecycle, organizations can identify environmental hotspots and optimize their designs to minimize the overall environmental impact. This can involve selecting more sustainable materials, optimizing manufacturing processes, or exploring alternative end-of-life scenarios, such as increased recyclability or reusability.

LCA can also support the development of innovative, eco-friendly products by providing insights into the environmental performance of different design options. This allows organizations to make informed decisions that balance product functionality, cost, and environmental sustainability, ultimately delivering products that meet the growing demand for sustainable solutions.

Main principles of Eco-design explained, and how those link to Life Cycle Stages and Environmental Life Cycle Assessment (LCA). What should you consider when you want to design a sustainable product or business model?

#### **Supply Chain Optimization**

Another key area where LCA can contribute to everyday decision support is in supply chain optimization. By analyzing the environmental impact of various suppliers, transportation modes, and logistics processes, organizations can identify opportunities to streamline their supply chains and reduce their carbon footprint.

For example, LCA can help organizations evaluate the environmental impact of different raw material sources, transportation routes, or packaging options. This information can then be used to select suppliers and logistics partners that align with the organization's sustainability goals, ultimately creating a more environmentally responsible and efficient supply chain.

#### **Strategic Planning and Goal-Setting**

LCA can also play a crucial role in strategic planning and goal-setting for organizations. By conducting comprehensive LCAs, organizations can gain a deeper understanding of their environmental impact across their entire value chain. This information can then be used to set meaningful, data-driven sustainability targets and develop strategies to achieve them.

For example, an organization may use LCA to identify the product categories or business activities that contribute the most to their overall environmental footprint. Armed with this knowledge, they can then prioritize initiatives and investments that will have the greatest impact on reducing their environmental impact, such as transitioning to renewable energy sources, implementing circular economy principles, or exploring alternative business models.

#### **Stakeholder Engagement and Reporting**

In addition to its internal decision-support capabilities, LCA can also be a valuable tool for engaging with external stakeholders, such as customers, investors, and regulatory bodies. By leveraging LCA data, organizations can communicate their environmental performance and sustainability efforts in a transparent and credible manner, building trust and enhancing their reputation as responsible corporate citizens.

LCA-based reporting, such as Environmental Product Declarations (EPDs) or Product Environmental Footprint (PEF) studies, can provide stakeholders with detailed, science-based information about the environmental impact of a product or service. This can help organizations differentiate themselves in the market, meet customer demands for sustainable products, and comply with evolving environmental regulations.

### Overcoming Barriers to LCA Adoption

While the benefits of incorporating LCA into everyday decision-making are clear, some organizations may still face barriers to adoption. Historically, LCA has been perceived as a complex and resource-intensive process, requiring specialized expertise and significant investments in software, data, and personnel.

However, the landscape is rapidly changing, and the barriers to LCA adoption are steadily diminishing. The availability of user-friendly LCA software, comprehensive databases, and online training resources has made it easier than ever before for organizations to build the necessary competencies and integrate LCA into their decision-making processes.

#### **Developing In-House LCA Expertise**

One of the key steps in overcoming the barriers to LCA adoption is to develop in-house expertise. This can be achieved through a combination of training, collaboration with external LCA experts, and the strategic hiring of sustainability professionals with LCA experience.

Organizations can leverage online courses, such as the Leader's LCA Online Course, to provide their employees with a comprehensive understanding of LCA concepts, methodologies, and applications. These courses can equip teams with the knowledge and skills necessary to conduct LCAs, interpret the results, and integrate the insights into their decision-making processes.

Additionally, organizations can collaborate with external LCA experts, either through consulting engagements or by establishing partnerships with research institutions or specialized LCA service providers. This can help organizations quickly build their LCA capabilities and gain access to the latest tools, data, and best practices.

#### **Leveraging User-Friendly LCA Software and Databases**

Another key factor in overcoming the barriers to LCA adoption is the availability of user-friendly LCA software and comprehensive databases. Gone are the days when LCA was confined to specialized software and limited data sources. Today, there are a wide range of LCA tools and databases that are designed to be accessible and intuitive, even for non-experts.

These software solutions, such as Earthster or OpenLCA, provide organizations with the necessary tools to conduct LCAs, manage data, and generate reports. They often come with pre-populated databases that include extensive environmental impact data for a wide range of materials, processes, and products, making it easier for organizations to get started with LCA without having to build their own comprehensive data sets from scratch.

#### **Integrating LCA into Existing Processes**

To ensure the successful integration of LCA into everyday decision-making, organizations should strive to embed the methodology into their existing processes and workflows. This may involve incorporating LCA considerations into product design reviews, supply chain management processes, or strategic planning initiatives.

By aligning LCA with existing decision-making frameworks, organizations can ensure that environmental impact assessments are not treated as a standalone exercise, but rather as an integral part of their overall business operations. This can help to drive the adoption of LCA throughout the organization and ensure that the insights generated are consistently applied to support more sustainable decision-making.

### Embracing the Future of Sustainable Decision-Making

As the world continues to grapple with the pressing challenges of climate change, resource depletion, and environmental degradation, the need for organizations to adopt more sustainable practices has never been more urgent. Life Cycle Assessment, with its ability to provide comprehensive, data-driven insights into the environmental impact of products and services, is poised to play a pivotal role in this transformation.

By embracing LCA as a day-to-day decision-support tool, organizations can unlock a wealth of benefits, from optimizing product design and supply chain operations to setting meaningful sustainability goals and engaging with stakeholders. With the right tools, resources, and expertise, LCA can be seamlessly integrated into the fabric of an organization's decision-making processes, empowering them to make informed choices that contribute to a more sustainable and circular future.

The time to act is now. By leveraging the power of Life Cycle Assessment, organizations can position themselves as leaders in the sustainability revolution, driving innovation, enhancing their competitive edge, and contributing to the collective effort to build a more resilient and environmentally responsible world.

### Dive Deeper with the Leader's LCA Online Course

If you're interested in expanding your knowledge and skills in Life Cycle Assessment, I highly recommend checking out the [Leader's LCA Online Course.](https://eu1.hubs.ly/H0b2XTC0) This comprehensive online course is designed to provide you with a deep understanding of LCA concepts, methodologies, and applications, empowering you to become a leader in the field of sustainability.

Gain fundamental insights into LCA and related concepts, such as GHG, EPD, and PEF

Learn the high-level process of conducting LCAs and identifying environmental impact hotspots

Explore real-world examples of LCA applications and their impact on decision-making

Prepare your organization for successful LCA implementation with the skills and knowledge acquired

[Read other participants’ reviews on the course](https://fi.trustpilot.com/review/environmentalacademy.org) and enroll now and unlock four hours of comprehensive content that will equip you with the tools and expertise to drive sustainable change within your organization. Don't miss this opportunity to become a leader in climate action and contribute to a more sustainable future.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Involving stakeholders in LCA ensures diverse perspectives, enhancing decision-making. This participation fosters transparency, builds trust, and aligns sustainability goals with community and market expectations.

Collaboration among stakeholders enhances LCA outcomes. By integrating insights from various sectors, organizations can identify shared goals, optimize resource use, and drive collective sustainability initiatives.

Implementing feedback mechanisms allows stakeholders to voice concerns and suggestions. This iterative process helps refine LCA methodologies, ensuring they remain relevant and responsive to evolving standards and expectations.
